proposals.novelty: While adversarial and self-play methods are known for training strong agents, their application specifically for learning a *prior policy* to replace MLE pre-training is novel. This bridges Jaques's work on adversarial training for coordination and modeling others with the core challenge of forming better initialization points for agent learning.

proposals.title: Learning to Learn a Prior: Joint Optimization of Prior and Policy for Sample-Efficient and Aligned Multi-Agent RL
proposals.motivation: In multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) and human-AI coordination, agents are often initialized with a fixed prior policy (e.g., from behavioral cloning or a pre-trained model) to guide exploration or ensure safety. However, a suboptimal or misaligned fixed prior can limit final performance or lead to inefficient training. This is a critical bottleneck for sample efficiency and alignment in complex, real-world interactive settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that jointly fine-tuning the prior policy alongside the RL policy during training will lead to more sample-efficient learning and higher final task performance than using a fixed prior, by dynamically adapting the exploration guide to the task and co-adapting agents.
proposals.method: We propose a bi-level optimization framework where an outer loop updates the parameters of a prior policy (e.g., a generative model or a policy network) and an inner loop runs a multi-agent RL algorithm (like MADDPG or QMIX) using the current prior for initialization or intrinsic reward shaping. The prior is updated to maximize the same long-term return as the RL policies, using meta-gradient techniques or a shared adversarial objective inspired by generative adversarial imitation learning. We will test this in mixed-motive multi-agent environments and a human-AI coordination testbed.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, flexible framework for learning adaptive priors in MARL, empirically demonstrating gains in sample efficiency and asymptotic performance. It will provide new insights into the co-adaptation of agents and the dynamic alignment of exploration strategies with task demands.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses fixed priors or meta-learns an initialization, jointly optimizing the prior and the policy within a single task is novel. This inverts the standard assumption, treating the prior as a learnable component of the training process itself.

proposals.title: Stabilizing On-Policy Policy Gradients with Off-Policy KL-Control Priors
proposals.motivation: On-policy policy gradient methods (e.g., PPO, A3C) are widely used for human-AI coordination and multi-agent training due to their stability, but suffer from high sample complexity. Conversely, off-policy methods with KL-control (e.g., as used in Maximum Entropy RL and some adversarial training setups) offer improved data efficiency and exploration but can be unstable in complex, multi-agent environments. Combining their strengths is a critical, unsolved challenge for sample-efficient and stable learning in interactive settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating an off-policy KL-control objective as a prior or a regularizer within an on-policy policy gradient framework will yield a hybrid algorithm that achieves superior sample efficiency and stability, particularly in multi-agent and human-in-the-loop tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel algorithm, KL-Prior Policy Optimization (KL-PPO), building on the PPO architecture. The method will maintain a replay buffer of off-policy data to train a separate 'prior' policy via KL-control (e.g., using a Soft Actor-Critic style objective). This prior will then be used to regularize the on-policy PPO loss via a forward KL divergence penalty, guiding the on-policy updates with efficient, exploratory knowledge. We will test KL-PPO in simulated multi-agent coordination environments and a human-AI cooperation task modeled as a two-player game.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a practical, unified algorithm that reduces the sample complexity of on-policy training for interactive agents. It will provide empirical evidence for the benefits of blending off-policy exploration with on-policy stability, advancing methods for training agents that must coordinate with humans or other AI systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has combined on- and off-policy elements (e.g., in IMPALA), the specific integration of an off-policy KL-control derived policy as a stabilizing prior for on-policy gradients is novel. This approach directly leverages Jaques's expertise in adversarial and multi-agent training where KL-control has proven useful.

proposals.title: Inverse Reward Inference via Multi-Agent Adversarial Imitation from Priors
proposals.motivation: Understanding the reward functions that drive AI policies is critical for alignment, interpretability, and safe human-AI coordination. Current inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) methods often assume a tabula rasa prior, ignoring the crucial role of pre-training or behavioral priors that shape final policy optimization. This limits our ability to audit or refine policies developed from foundation models or via multi-agent interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly modeling a known policy prior and using a multi-agent adversarial framework, we can more accurately and efficiently infer the implicit reward function that explains the transformation from that prior to a given final policy.
proposals.method: We will frame the problem as a two-player game between a reward network and a policy network. The policy network is initialized with the known prior policy (e.g., a pre-trained model or a behavioral clone). The reward network is trained to produce rewards such that the final target policy is optimal, while the policy network is adversarially fine-tuned from the prior towards the final policy under these rewards. We will adapt adversarial IRL (AIRL) and generative adversarial imitation learning (GAIL) architectures, incorporating the prior as a fixed starting point in the policy optimization loop, and test this in multi-agent coordination environments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new method for reward inference that accounts for initialization, making it more applicable to real-world systems built on pre-trained models. It will contribute to the fields of interpretable RL and AI alignment by offering a tool to audit the objective functions learned during fine-tuning or multi-agent training.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard IRL, which infers reward from trajectories alone, our approach uniquely conditions the inference process on a known policy prior, integrating it directly into the adversarial optimization. This bridges the gap between prior-aware policy optimization and inverse reward learning.

proposals.title: Adversarial Retrieval-Augmented Meta-Learning for Robust and Sample-Efficient Human-AI Personalization
proposals.motivation: Personalizing AI systems to individual human users is crucial for effective collaboration, but is often data-inefficient and brittle. Current few-shot personalization methods can overfit to sparse demonstrations, failing to generalize robustly. This work addresses the core challenge of building systems that are both faithful to a user's unique style and robust to novel situations, a key problem in human-AI coordination.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that framing personalization as an adversarial retrieval and composition task will yield systems that are more robust to distributional shift and more sample-efficient than standard fine-tuning or meta-learning alone, by explicitly leveraging a broad corpus of learned behavioral patterns.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage framework. First, a foundation policy or model is pre-trained on a large, multi-user interaction dataset. Second, a retrieval system indexes this dataset or its latent representations for behavioral patterns. For a new user, their few demonstrations query this index; the retrieved patterns are composed and used to initialize a model. A meta-learning algorithm (e.g., MAML) performs rapid adaptation, while an adversarial component (inspired by generative adversarial imitation learning) trains the retriever and adapter to produce policies robust to perturbations not present in the seed demonstrations.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, unified framework for sample-efficient personalization that combines retrieval, meta-learning, and adversarial robustness. It will provide new benchmarks for evaluating the robustness of personalized AI systems and advance methods for human-AI coordination by improving an agent's ability to generalize from minimal human data.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of adversarial training with retrieval-augmented meta-learning for personalization is non-obvious. While retrieval-augmented generation exists in NLP and adversarial training is used for robustness, their combined application to learn robust, composable behavioral representations from few human demonstrations is unexplored.

proposals.title: VaccineRL: Bootstrapping Personalized AI with Sparse Immunological Memory Modules
proposals.motivation: Current AI personalization relies on continuous, large-scale data collection, raising privacy concerns and computational costs. This paradigm is inefficient, failing to mimic how humans learn personalized behaviors from sparse, critical interactions. Drawing from immunology and multi-agent learning offers a novel path to sample-efficient, privacy-preserving personalization.
proposals.hypothesis: That a user's initial small demonstration can act as a 'personalization antigen' to initialize a sparse, user-specific memory module within a neural network, enabling rapid, context-aware adaptation upon encountering analogous future inputs, similar to a secondary immune response.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-agent reinforcement learning framework where a primary 'global' agent interacts with a sparse, user-specific 'memory' agent (the immunological module). The memory agent, initialized via meta-learning on the user's demonstration batch, remains dormant. An adversarial discriminator network will be trained to detect inputs analogous to the initial data, triggering the memory agent's activation. The system will be trained using adversarial and intrinsic reward signals to reinforce effective, personalized responses only when the memory pathway is activated, preventing catastrophic forgetting of global knowledge.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel, biologically-inspired architecture for sample-efficient AI personalization that minimizes persistent data collection. It will contribute a new training paradigm combining meta-initialization, adversarial triggering, and multi-agent credit assignment to create sparse, persistent personalization. The results will advance methods for human-AI coordination and lifelong learning.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the immunological analogy operationalized for AI: treating minimal user data as a vaccine to create a sparse, conditionally-activated memory module, rather than performing standard fine-tuning. This integrates adversarial triggering with multi-agent RL for personalization in a way not explored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Disentangling and Defending: Adversarially Robust Multi-Agent Preference Modeling with Graph-Based Provenance
proposals.motivation: Modern recommender and content systems model user preferences in latent spaces, but these representations are vulnerable to pollution. Coordinated inauthentic behavior and algorithmic feedback loops can create spurious correlations, distorting models of social influence and leading to harmful filter bubbles or manipulation. Pre-2026, methods for robustly disentangling organic social dynamics from adversarial signals in multi-agent preference data remain underdeveloped.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly modeling the provenance of preference data as a graph and incorporating an adversarial training regime that penalizes representations predictable from known inauthentic coordination patterns, we can learn a latent space that robustly disentangles intrinsic preferences, genuine social influence, and adversarial correlation components.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-agent adversarial training framework. A primary encoder learns a disentangled latent representation from user interaction sequences. A graph neural network processes provenance data (e.g., follower networks, co-engagement patterns) to model social influence pathways. An adversarial 'correlation attacker' network, trained on known inauthentic coordination subgraphs, attempts to predict latent dimensions. The primary model is trained with a combined loss: reconstruction of observed preferences, a contrastive loss for genuine social graph alignment, and an adversarial penalty minimizing predictability by the attacker, inspired by adversarial inverse reinforcement learning and generative adversarial network principles.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, robust framework for modeling multi-agent preferences resilient to coordinated manipulation. It will provide a more interpretable decomposition of preference formation and yield new metrics for assessing the integrity of learned social influence in latent spaces, advancing the field of human-AI coordination and robust multi-agent learning.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the integration of graph-based provenance data directly into the adversarial training loop to defend the latent space, moving beyond standard adversarial robustness which focuses on input perturbations to instead target corruption of the learned correlation structure itself.

proposals.title: Adversarial Preference Games: Robust Multi-Agent Learning for Calibrated Human Feedback
proposals.motivation: Personalized AI systems rely on user feedback, but such feedback is often noisy, inconsistent, or context-dependent, leading to poor model alignment. Pre-2026 methods in preference learning and reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) often treat user responses as uniformly reliable, which limits robustness and sample efficiency in real-world human-AI interaction.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling a user's latent preference and their response reliability as separate, inferable variables within a multi-agent adversarial framework, an AI system can more efficiently and robustly learn personalized policies that are resilient to noisy or strategic feedback.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-agent system with a Preference Inference Agent and a Reliability Discriminator Agent. The Preference Agent uses a Bayesian neural network to maintain a posterior over user preferences. The Discriminator, trained adversarially, estimates a user-specific reliability score for each query response. An active query strategy, formulated as a Thompson sampling or information gain problem over this joint latent space, will select queries that optimally reduce uncertainty in both preference and reliability. Training will use a modified multi-agent RL objective, drawing on pre-2026 adversarial training techniques from generative modeling and coordination literature.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, sample-efficient algorithm for robust preference elicitation that explicitly accounts for user reliability. It will advance the field of human-in-the-loop RL by providing a formal framework to integrate and weight explicit feedback against implicit behavioral signals, leading to more calibrated and trustworthy personalization.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the adversarial, multi-agent formulation of the joint inference problem, treating reliability not as a static heuristic but as a learnable component in a strategic interaction, which connects calibrated preference learning with pre-2026 advances in adversarial training for human-AI coordination.

proposals.title: Socially-Aware Active Preference Learning via Multi-Agent Bayesian Optimization
proposals.motivation: Personalizing AI systems to individual human preferences is crucial for human-AI coordination, but current active learning methods treat users as isolated agents, requiring a high query burden for each new user. This is inefficient and fails to leverage the inherent correlations and social structures within user populations. A method that strategically queries users to learn both individual preferences and the population's relational structure would enable rapid, scalable personalization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an active learning framework which models users as a multi-agent system embedded in a shared latent space, and which uses a novel acquisition function to jointly optimize for individual and social information gain, will achieve significantly higher sample efficiency in preference learning than isolated or naive collaborative methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-agent Bayesian optimization framework where each user is an agent with a latent preference vector. A graph neural network will model the correlational structure between users. The core innovation is a new acquisition function for query selection, combining Expected Improvement for a target user's preference with a graph-aware Information Gain term that quantifies the reduction in uncertainty about the population's latent structure. This function will be optimized using multi-agent reinforcement learning to learn a strategic querying policy.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, socially-aware active learning framework for preference elicitation, 2) a new class of acquisition functions for Bayesian optimization that balance individual and population-level exploration, and 3) empirical evidence that modeling social structure accelerates human-AI coordination and personalization in simulated and real-world tasks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit fusion of multi-agent relational modeling with Bayesian active learning, framing query selection as a reinforcement learning problem that reasons across a population, moving beyond treating users independently or using static, pre-defined similarity measures.

proposals.title: Negotiating Alignment: A Multi-Agent Adversarial Framework for Context-Sensitive Human Feedback
proposals.motivation: Current AI alignment via Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) assumes a monolithic 'human' preference, risking marginalization of minority values and poor adaptation to cultural or situational context. This is a critical problem for deploying AI assistants globally and in diverse social settings. A more robust approach must explicitly model and negotiate value pluralism.
proposals.hypothesis: An AI system trained via multi-agent adversarial learning, where distinct agents represent different human value models derived from participatory panels, will achieve more equitable and context-aware alignment than systems trained on aggregated feedback.
proposals.method: We will first conduct structured participatory design workshops to create a taxonomy of values and contexts. This taxonomy will train multiple reward models, each representing a distinct value profile. These models will act as adversaries in a multi-agent RL setup, where a central 'negotiation' policy (meta-learned using methods like MAML) must satisfy a context-weighted combination of their rewards. The context (e.g., inferred user demographics, conversation topic) will be provided via a multimodal encoder (text, audio, potentially video) and used to modulate the adversarial objective.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a novel technical framework for value-pluralistic alignment, moving beyond single-reward RLHF. It provides a concrete methodology for integrating participatory social science into AI training pipelines. The resulting system is a step towards AI that can dynamically adapt its alignment goals based on the interaction context.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is framing value alignment as a multi-agent adversarial negotiation problem guided by empirically derived value models, rather than optimizing a single proxy reward. This directly integrates participatory design and adversarial training—core themes in Jaques' prior work—into the alignment problem.

proposals.title: Adversarial Theory-Grounded Preference Learning: A Multi-Agent Framework for Interpretable Latent Structure Discovery
proposals.motivation: Current preference learning models, while powerful, often produce latent representations that are uninterpretable or fail to generalize, limiting their utility for human-AI coordination and alignment. This is a critical gap, as robust models of human preference are needed for trustworthy AI assistants and recommendation systems. A purely data-driven approach lacks the constraints necessary to yield structures that are both semantically meaningful and robust to distributional shift.
proposals.hypothesis: By coupling a generative agent that proposes latent structures grounded in formal theories (e.g., Maslow's hierarchy, prospect theory) with an adversarial agent that critiques their predictive and semantic coherence, we can discover preference models that are more interpretable, robust, and generalizable than those from purely inductive methods.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-agent reinforcement learning framework. A Generator agent, using a structured variational autoencoder, proposes hierarchical or non-Gaussian latent models whose priors and architectures are constrained by templates from psychological/economic theory. A Discriminator agent adversarially evaluates these models on two fronts: a temporal predictor network assesses fitness on sequential user behavior, while a semantic critic network, potentially using pre-2026 language models, evaluates alignment of latent dimensions with theoretical constructs. The agents are trained via a combined reward signal.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for theory-guided, interpretable machine learning that balances inductive and deductive reasoning. It will advance methods for human-AI coordination by producing more robust and explainable models of human preferences. The adversarial evaluation protocol provides a new benchmark for validating the semantic validity of learned latent spaces.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the explicit, adversarial integration of formal theoretical constraints from other fields into the structure discovery process, moving beyond regularization or post-hoc interpretation. The multi-agent setup directly operationalizes the 'modeling others' minds' concept by having agents reason about and critique theory-laden mental models.

proposals.title: HyperPref: Meta-Learning a Hypernetwork for Unbounded and Adaptive Human Preference Modeling
proposals.motivation: Effective human-AI collaboration requires AI agents to rapidly adapt to diverse and evolving human preferences. Current methods, such as training discrete reward model ensembles or fine-tuning on new users, are sample-inefficient, prone to catastrophic forgetting, and cannot scale to an unbounded number of preference modes. This limits the deployment of personalized agents in real-world, multi-user settings.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-learned hypernetwork, which generates reward model parameters conditioned on a continuous latent mode vector, can enable fast, few-shot adaptation to new human preferences without catastrophic interference, effectively scaling to an unbounded number of preference modes.
proposals.method: We will implement a hypernetwork that outputs the weights of a reward model neural network, conditioned on a latent mode vector z. This system will be meta-trained using a Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) objective across a diverse, procedurally generated distribution of simulated human preference modes (e.g., in cooperative games). For a new user, a few interaction trajectories will be used to infer their z via gradient-based inference on the meta-learned loss, after which the hypernetwork generates their personalized reward model. We will evaluate adaptation speed and final coordination performance against baselines like multi-task RL and fine-tuning.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a scalable, sample-efficient framework for personalized reward learning that avoids catastrophic interference. It will advance the field of human-AI coordination by providing a principled method for agents to adapt online to a theoretically unbounded variety of human partners, moving beyond pre-defined user clusters.
proposals.novelty: The approach uniquely combines hypernetworks for unbounded mode scalability with meta-learning for few-shot adaptation, moving beyond discrete clustering of preferences (e.g., behavioral cloning mixtures) or rigid multi-task frameworks that cannot generalize to new modes.

proposals.title: Robust Preference Modeling via Adversarial Mixture-of-Experts Training
proposals.motivation: Reward modeling is critical for aligning AI systems with complex, multimodal human preferences, but learned reward functions are vulnerable to adversarial 'reward hacking' by RL agents. Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models can capture diverse preferences but may be brittle, where an agent can discover and exploit a single expert's simplistic reward signal. This fragility undermines the safety and reliability of aligned AI systems.
proposals.hypothesis: Co-training a MoE reward model against a dedicated adversarial policy agent will force the MoE to develop robust, non-obvious routing logic and reward signals, resulting in a reward model that maintains its nuanced representation of preferences while being significantly more resistant to adversarial manipulation.
proposals.method: We will implement a two-player adversarial game. The first player is a MoE reward model, where a gating network routes state-action pairs to specialized expert networks. The second player is an adversarial RL agent trained to maximize the MoE's predicted reward via policy gradients (e.g., PPO), thereby actively seeking to 'hack' it. They are trained concurrently: the MoE is updated to minimize the correlation between the adversarial agent's trajectories and true human preference labels (using a cross-entropy loss), while the adversarial agent is updated to maximize the MoE's output. This forces the MoE's gating and experts to rely on robust, multimodal features.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, adversarially robust reward modeling framework that directly addresses the reward hacking problem. It will contribute new evaluation metrics for reward model robustness and demonstrate improved alignment stability in downstream RL fine-tuning tasks, advancing the reliability of human-AI coordination.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial training and MoE architectures exist, their combination for hardening reward models against strategic policy manipulation is novel. The core innovation is framing reward robustness as a dynamic, two-player game where the adversary actively learns to exploit the MoE's structure, moving beyond static dataset perturbations.

proposals.title: Meta-Training a Bayesian Prior for Robust and Uncertainty-Aware Adaptation in Human-Agent Teams
proposals.motivation: Personalized AI assistants must adapt quickly to individual users, but current methods often overfit to sparse or noisy feedback, leading to misalignment. Pre-2026 meta-learning for fast adaptation lacks principled uncertainty quantification, while Bayesian models are often too slow for real-time interaction. This creates a critical gap for safe, reliable personalization in high-stakes domains like education or healthcare.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that meta-training a hierarchical Bayesian prior over user reward functions, coupled with an amortized variational inference network, will enable faster, more robust, and uncertainty-aware online adaptation than standard meta-reinforcement learning or non-hierarchical Bayesian methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a meta-learning framework where the outer loop trains a global prior distribution over the parameters of user-specific reward models using a population of simulated users. The inner loop will use an amortized inference network, based on Stochastic Gradient Variational Bayes, to perform fast posterior updates from a user's interaction history. We will integrate this with a multi-agent adversarial training paradigm, where a 'user simulator' agent generates diverse and challenging adaptation scenarios to robustify the meta-prior.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, unified algorithm for uncertainty-aware meta-adaptation, providing both theoretical grounding in hierarchical Bayesian inference and practical efficiency. It will advance the field of human-AI coordination by demonstrating how explicit uncertainty modeling can guide clarification requests and safe fallback behaviors in real-time interaction.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the integration of meta-learning to learn a Bayesian prior specifically for efficient online inference, moving beyond point estimates, combined with adversarial training for robustness—a synthesis not present in pre-2026 Bayesian RL or meta-RL literature.

proposals.title: Meta-Learning Socially-Aware Adaptation for Sample-Efficient Human-AI Coordination
proposals.motivation: Personalized AI systems, from recommender systems to collaborative agents, require rapid adaptation to individual users, but often face a data sparsity problem. Current meta-learning approaches for personalization treat users as independent, ignoring the rich social and correlational structure within a population that could inform priors for faster inference. Leveraging this structure is crucial for developing AI partners that can quickly align with a new human's preferences and mental model, a core challenge in human-AI coordination.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a meta-learner, trained with a structured prior over a latent preference space that explicitly models correlations and social influences between users, will achieve more sample-efficient and robust adaptation to new individuals than methods using generic or independent priors.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) framework where each 'agent' represents a user's latent preference model. A graph neural network (GNN) will encode the relational structure between users (derived from explicit social networks or implicit similarity) to parameterize a structured Gaussian Process or normalizing flow prior. A meta-reinforcement learning algorithm (e.g., RL^2 or a gradient-based meta-learner like MAML) will be trained to rapidly infer a new user's latent parameters by querying this socially-informed prior and performing Bayesian updates, with the adaptation policy itself being learned. The system will be evaluated in simulated human-AI coordination environments (e.g., Overcooked, Hanabi) and preference learning benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, unified framework for sample-efficient personalization that integrates structured population modeling with meta-learning. It will advance methods for human-AI coordination by enabling agents to build a 'theory of mind' for new partners more quickly. The research will also provide insights into how social and correlational priors can be formally embedded into adaptive learning systems.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the fusion of a socially-structured population prior with a meta-learned adaptation strategy, moving beyond treating users as independent or using unstructured priors. This explicitly models how inferences about one user should directly and dynamically inform inferences about another, which is non-trivial to learn end-to-end.

proposals.title: Meta-Learning Latent Reward Models for Fast Adaptation in Human-AI Coordination
proposals.motivation: Effective human-AI coordination requires AI agents to understand and adapt to diverse human preferences, which are often latent and costly to infer from scratch. Pre-2026 methods for learning reward models from human feedback, such as inverse reinforcement learning, are sample-inefficient when encountering new users, limiting the scalability of personalized AI assistants and collaborative agents. This creates a bottleneck for deploying adaptable agents in real-world, multi-user environments.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-learned reward model, trained across a distribution of simulated or human users, can rapidly adapt to a new user's latent preferences with minimal interaction data, significantly improving initial coordination performance compared to non-adaptive or fine-tuned baselines.
proposals.method: We propose a model-agnostic meta-learning (MAML) framework where the inner loop adapts a reward network's parameters to a user's inferred latent vector (e.g., from a generative model of human behavior or few-shot demonstrations). The outer loop meta-trains the reward model's initial parameters across many simulated user tasks, optimizing for fast adaptation. We will test this in multi-agent reinforcement learning environments requiring coordination, using adversarial self-play to generate diverse training tasks and robust latent spaces.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a sample-efficient method for personalizing AI behavior, directly advancing human-in-the-loop reinforcement learning. It will provide empirical evidence on the transfer of meta-learned reward structures across users and a benchmark for fast adaptation in cooperative AI settings.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning has been applied to RL policies, its direct application to learn an adaptable *reward model* for human latent inference is novel. This combines Jaques's expertise in adversarial multi-agent training and modeling human minds with meta-learning for a new, user-adaptive coordination paradigm.

proposals.title: Diffusion Reward Modeling: Capturing Multimodal Preferences for Human-Agent Coordination
proposals.motivation: Inverse Reinforcement Learning (IRL) and reward learning from human feedback are central to aligning AI agents with human intent. However, existing methods often assume a unimodal or unimodal-like reward function, failing to capture the true complexity and ambiguity of human preferences, which are often multimodal. This limitation hinders robust human-AI coordination and sample-efficient policy learning in complex, open-ended tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling the reward function as a diffusion process—a state-of-the-art generative model for complex, structured distributions—will enable more accurate and sample-efficient learning of multimodal human preference landscapes, leading to improved agent performance and coordination in multi-agent and human-in-the-loop settings.
proposals.method: We will develop a Diffusion Reward Model (DRM) that treats the reward function as a denoising diffusion probabilistic model (DDPM) conditioned on state-action pairs or trajectories. The model will be trained on human preference data (e.g., pairwise comparisons) or demonstrations using a noise-prediction objective. For policy learning, we will integrate the DRM's learned multimodal density into a Maximum Entropy RL or adversarial training framework (e.g., using a modified GAIL) to allow agents to reason over diverse reward hypotheses, drawing on Jaques's prior work on multi-agent RL and adversarial training for coordination.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, generative formulation for reward learning that captures multimodality, 2) a practical algorithm for integrating diffusion-based rewards into policy optimization, and 3) empirical validation in multi-agent coordination and human-AI teaming benchmarks, demonstrating improved sample efficiency and robustness to preference ambiguity.
proposals.novelty: While diffusion models are established for image generation, their application to directly model the reward function space for RL is novel. This approach moves beyond typical scalar or Gaussian reward models to a full generative density, directly addressing the core challenge of preference ambiguity that simpler models overlook.

ideas.title: Adversarially Robust Mixture-of-Experts for Reward Modeling
ideas.description: This approach applies a mixture-of-experts architecture to model diverse human preferences but crucially hardens it against adversarial policy manipulation. The core innovation is the co-training of the MoE reward model against a dedicated adversarial agent that actively attempts to discover and exploit weaknesses in the expert routing and reward assignment. This process forces the MoE to develop robust, non-obvious routing logic and reward signals that cannot be easily inverted or gamed. The resulting reward model is more reliable for aligning AI systems, as it maintains its nuanced multimodal representation while being resistant to reward hacking.

ideas.title: Retrieval-Augmented Meta-Learning for Robust Personalization from Minimal Demonstrations
ideas.description: This approach addresses the data insufficiency flaw by framing personalization as a retrieval and composition problem. A foundation model, pre-trained on a large, anonymized dataset of interactions from many users, is paired with a retrieval system that indexes learned behavioral or preference patterns. A user's few demonstrations act as a query to retrieve the most relevant patterns, which are then composed and fine-tuned via a meta-learning algorithm to rapidly adapt the foundation model to the user. This results in a personalized system that is both faithful to the user's seed data and robustly generalizable, as it leverages broader, learned structures from the pre-training corpus.

ideas.title: Apply conservative Q-learning from robotics
ideas.description: Use Conservative Q-Learning (CQL) to address overestimation in offline RL for large vocabulary dialog.
ideas.sourceTitle: Human-centric Dialog Training via Offline Reinforcement Learning

ideas.title: Use distributional RL for reward uncertainty
ideas.description: Model the full distribution of returns to better quantify uncertainty in language generation rewards.
ideas.sourceTitle: Human-centric Dialog Training via Offline Reinforcement Learning

ideas.title: Explore more sophisticated self-play reward functions
ideas.description: Investigate combining the proposed proxies with other conversational quality signals for self-play evaluation.
ideas.sourceTitle: Approximating Interactive Human Evaluation with Self-Play for Open-Domain Dialog Systems

ideas.title: Meta-Learning a Hypernetwork for Unbounded and Adaptive Preference Modes
ideas.description: This research direction develops a meta-learned hypernetwork that generates the parameters of a reward model conditioned on a continuous latent mode vector. The architecture is designed to scale to an extremely large or effectively unbounded number of modes by leveraging the continuous latent space. Crucially, the meta-learning objective trains the hypernetwork such that, given a few interactions from a new user, it can rapidly infer their latent mode and generate a personalized reward model, achieving fast adaptation without catastrophic interference or the need for pre-defined discrete clusters.

ideas.title: Preference-Guided Sufficient Explanations for Interactive Model Refinement
ideas.description: This research direction develops methods to identify and refine a model's minimal sufficient explanations for its predictions based on direct human preference feedback. Instead of treating explanations as static, the system treats them as hypotheses that can be adjusted—by adding, removing, or re-weighting features—in response to mixed feedback like corrections, demonstrations, or natural language instructions stating a preference for a different explanatory style or focus. This enables a collaborative loop where humans critique not just the prediction, but the sufficiency and acceptability of the explanation itself, guiding the model to adopt more aligned and parsimonious reasoning. The result is a model that becomes more interpretable and trustworthy by converging on explanations that are both minimally sufficient for the task and satisfactory to the user.

ideas.title: Incorporate meta-learning for fast user adaptation
ideas.description: Apply meta-learning to quickly adapt the reward model to a new user's inferred latent.
ideas.sourceTitle: Personalizing Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback with Variational Preference Learning

ideas.title: MoE Attribution for Debugging and Aligning Reward Models
ideas.description: This research combines the diagnostic power of reliable feature attribution with the modular architecture of a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) reward model. The core idea is to use attribution techniques not on the final policy model, but internally on the reward model's experts to debug why it produces poor or misaligned reward signals. When the reward model fails on certain inputs, attribution can pinpoint which expert(s) are responsible and, crucially, which features they incorrectly attended to. This allows for targeted debugging—such as retraining a single faulty expert on corrected data or pruning misleading features—leading to more efficient and interpretable alignment of reward models with complex, multimodal human preferences.

ideas.title: Apply meta-learning for fast adaptation to partners
ideas.description: Enable dialog agents to quickly adapt their policy to different user styles.
ideas.sourceTitle: Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Open-Domain Dialog

ideas.title: Extract more diverse reward functions post-hoc

ideas.sourceTitle: Way Off-Policy Batch Deep Reinforcement Learning of Implicit Human Preferences in Dialog

ideas.title: Explore more implicit conversational reward signals

ideas.sourceTitle: Human-centric Dialog Training via Offline Reinforcement Learning

ideas.title: Participatory Value Pluralism Framework for Context-Aware Multimodal Human Feedback Alignment
ideas.description: This research develops a system to align AI with diverse human values by first explicitly mapping and negotiating value pluralism across different populations and contexts. It employs participatory design with cross-cultural panels to create a structured taxonomy of values and contextual factors, which then informs the training of multiple, context-sensitive reward models. These models guide a meta-learned multimodal policy that can adapt its alignment objectives based on the detected cultural, demographic, and situational context of the interaction, moving beyond a one-size-fits-all 'human feedback' signal to a more robust, equitable, and negotiated alignment process.

ideas.title: Non-Linear Human Feedback Attribution for Robust and Culturally-Aware Alignment
ideas.description: This research develops and deploys novel attribution methods that bypass local linear approximations to analyze multimodal RLHF training on large-scale, real-world alignment tasks. By directly modeling the non-linear pathways through which diverse human feedback influences model updates, the approach can pinpoint why a model fails to align with specific cultural or demographic subgroups. This enables the creation of more robust, auditable, and equitable alignment pipelines that can be diagnostically improved, moving beyond aggregate performance metrics to understand and rectify disparate model behaviors.

ideas.title: Apply options framework from hierarchical RL

ideas.sourceTitle: Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Open-Domain Dialog

ideas.title: Inverse Attribution for Dynamic Preference Alignment
ideas.description: This research addresses the challenge of aligning AI systems with users whose preferences evolve, by solving a temporal inverse attribution problem. Given a user's desired profile for how much their current outputs should be attributed to their long-term static preferences versus recent dynamic shifts, the system must regularize its online latent inference and policy updates to produce that exact attribution balance. This moves beyond merely adapting to preferences, to explicitly controlling the *perceived source* of model behavior—fostering appropriate trust and allowing users to dictate the plasticity of their AI assistant. The result is a policy that not only tracks changing preferences but does so in a way that is interpretably aligned with the user's meta-preferences about stability and change.

ideas.title: Inverse: Infer reward from prior and policy
ideas.description: Given a prior and a final policy, infer the implicit reward function that was optimized.
ideas.sourceTitle: Sequence Tutor: Conservative Fine-Tuning of Sequence Generation Models with KL-control

ideas.title: Meta-Learning with Hierarchical Bayesian Inference for Robust and Uncertainty-Aware User Adaptation
ideas.description: This system combines meta-learning with a hierarchical Bayesian model to enable robust, uncertainty-aware adaptation to individual users. It meta-trains a prior distribution over reward model parameters and an efficient online inference procedure, allowing it to maintain a belief state over user preferences that updates with each interaction. By explicitly modeling uncertainty, the system can identify when user feedback is ambiguous or insufficient, triggering requests for clarification or falling back to safer, more conservative behavior. This approach ensures adaptation is not only fast but also reliable, avoiding overconfident misalignment from limited or noisy data.

ideas.title: Meta-Learning a Socially-Aware Latent Preference Prior for Rapid, Contextual User Adaptation
ideas.description: This research direction develops a meta-learning framework where the adaptation process for a new user's latent preference parameters is guided by a learned prior over the population's latent space that explicitly models correlations and social influences between users. Instead of adapting from a generic or independent prior, the meta-learner is trained to rapidly infer a new user's preferences by leveraging how their latent traits relate to, or are influenced by, clusters or networks of existing users. This results in a system that can personalize with extremely sparse data by making intelligent, socially-contextualized inferences, effectively blending fast adaptation with a structured understanding of preference interdependence. The core novelty lies in meta-learning the adaptation strategy itself to optimally utilize this structured prior, achieving more robust and accurate personalization than either fast adaptation or population modeling alone.

ideas.title: Apply curriculum learning strategies

ideas.title: Challenge self-play as sufficient for human likeness
ideas.description: A model talking to itself may not capture nuances of human conversational strategies and goals.
ideas.sourceTitle: Approximating Interactive Human Evaluation with Self-Play for Open-Domain Dialog Systems

ideas.title: Formal Desiderata for Reliable Attribution in Data-Constrained Personalization
ideas.description: This research direction establishes formal properties that attribution methods must satisfy to be considered reliable specifically in the context of personalization bootstrapped from tiny user-specific datasets. It moves beyond generic desiderata by defining conditions—such as stability under low-data fine-tuning and faithfulness to personalized model decisions—that ensure feature attributions remain consistent and meaningful after model adaptation. The synergy enables the development of attribution techniques that can audit and justify personalized model behavior even when the personalization process itself is data-starved and statistically unstable. This provides a foundation for trustworthy, explainable AI systems that can safely adapt to individual users.

ideas.title: Immunological Memory for AI Personalization
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that effective personalization requires continuous large-scale data collection. Drawing an analogy from immunology, where a vaccine uses a tiny, controlled antigen exposure to bootstrap a lifelong, adaptive immune memory, we propose a 'Vaccination for Personalization' method. A user's initial small demonstration or feedback batch is treated as a 'personalization antigen.' Instead of merely fine-tuning a global model, this data is used to initialize a sparse, user-specific 'memory module' within the AI system. This module remains quiescent until the system encounters inputs analogous to the initial demonstration. When triggered, it activates and rapidly adapts the system's response, mimicking the secondary immune response. This allows for strong, context-aware personalization to be bootstrapped from minimal initial data, with the system building its understanding efficiently over time based on selective activation and reinforcement of these memory pathways.

ideas.title: Scale down: PAIRED for sample-efficient meta-RL
ideas.description: Use the UED framework to quickly generate a minimal, informative set of training tasks.
ideas.sourceTitle: Emergent Complexity and Zero-shot Transfer via Unsupervised Environment Design

ideas.title: Challenge MLE pre-training as the optimal prior

ideas.sourceTitle: Sequence Tutor: Conservative Fine-Tuning of Sequence Generation Models with KL-control

ideas.title: Theory-Grounded Discovery of Interpretable Latent Preference Structures
ideas.description: This research develops a framework that couples the flexible discovery of complex latent preference structures with strong theory-driven constraints to ensure robustness and interpretability. Instead of purely data-driven exploration, the system proposes candidate non-Gaussian or hierarchical latent models that are explicitly linked to pre-existing psychological, economic, or sociological theories of human preference and decision-making. The performance and validity of these structures are then adversarially evaluated based on both predictive accuracy on temporal user behavior and the semantic coherence of the learned latent dimensions with the proposed theoretical constructs. The final output is a more robust, generalizable, and explainable model of intricate preference relationships that balances inductive power with deductive reasoning.

ideas.title: Active Preference Learning with Socially-Aware Bayesian Querying
ideas.description: This research direction develops an active learning framework for user preferences that explicitly models and exploits the dependencies between users within a population. Instead of querying users in isolation, the system strategically selects queries that are maximally informative for both the target user's latent preference and the underlying social or correlational structure of the broader user community. This allows the system to leverage information gained from one user to dramatically reduce the query burden for others, enabling rapid, data-efficient personalization at scale. The core challenge involves designing novel acquisition functions for Bayesian optimization that balance exploration of an individual's preference space with exploration of the population's relational graph in a shared latent representation.

ideas.title: Calibrated Preference Elicitation via Joint Inference of Latent Preference and User Response Reliability
ideas.description: This system actively queries users while simultaneously learning to interpret the reliability of their responses. It employs a Bayesian model that jointly infers a user's core latent preference and a personal reliability parameter, which captures how consistently their stated preferences align with their observed choices and interactions. The active learning strategy optimizes a dual objective: reducing uncertainty about the true preference and refining the reliability model to discount low-quality feedback. This results in robust personalization that gracefully integrates explicit but potentially noisy user input with implicit behavioral signals.

ideas.title: Adversarially Robust Modeling of Social Influence in Preference Latent Spaces
ideas.description: This research develops a method to model user preference correlations while defending against adversarial manipulation of the latent space. The system disentangles latent factors into organic social influence, individual intrinsic preference, and adversarial correlation components using a combination of graph-based provenance data and adversarial training. It actively penalizes the learning of representations that are predictable from known inauthentic coordination patterns, ensuring the model captures genuine preference dynamics. The outcome is a more robust and interpretable latent space that resists pollution from coordinated inauthentic behavior or algorithmic feedback loops.

ideas.title: Scale down to minimal viable social learning

ideas.title: Adapting Participatory Action Research for Multimodal RLHF with Diverse Populations
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from community psychology. The analogous problem of developing effective, sustainable, and ethical interventions within diverse human populations is often solved through Participatory Action Research (PAR), where research is embedded in and co-created with communities. Adapting this, the research idea proposes to deploy multimodal RLHF not as a one-off large-scale data scrape, but through structured, equitable partnerships with specific, diverse human populations. Community members would become active collaborators in defining what 'alignment' means for their context, designing the feedback interfaces (leveraging multimodal inputs), and iteratively refining the AI system, ensuring the alignment process is robust, culturally informed, and addresses real-world needs as defined by the users themselves.

ideas.title: Apply meta-learning for fast personalization

ideas.description: Test the Way Off-Policy algorithm in domains beyond dialog, like recommendation systems or educational tutoring.

ideas.title: Approximate Hierarchical Attribution for Complex Latent Preference Models
ideas.description: This research direction develops novel feature attribution methods specifically designed for models that capture complex, hierarchical latent preference structures (e.g., via Bayesian nonparametrics or deep latent variable models). Instead of enforcing the strict completeness axiom, which requires attributions to sum precisely to the model output, we propose and formalize a relaxed, approximate completeness condition. This relaxation is crucial because the complex interactions and non-Gaussian distributions within the latent space make exact, additive decomposition of the output often intractable or semantically meaningless. The resulting framework provides stable, interpretable attributions that quantify both the contribution of input features and the uncertainty introduced by the model's intricate latent preference hierarchy, bridging advanced preference modeling with robust, practical explainability.

ideas.title: Batch data is static and fully collected
ideas.description: Allow the batch to be incrementally updated with new human interactions during training.
ideas.sourceTitle: Way Off-Policy Batch Deep Reinforcement Learning of Implicit Human Preferences in Dialog

ideas.title: Robust Online Sensitivity Analysis for Evolving Preference Latents
ideas.description: This research direction develops methods for performing online, robust sensitivity analysis within dynamic latent variable models of user preference. By integrating influence function techniques from robust statistics into the online inference loop, the system can continuously assess how sensitive the current estimate of a user's latent preferences is to each new piece of feedback. This allows for the automatic detection of outliers, temporary mood shifts, or adversarial data, enabling the policy adaptation mechanism to robustly differentiate between genuine preference evolution and noise. Consequently, the system achieves more stable and trustworthy personalization by adapting its learning rate and update rules based on the statistical influence of incoming observations.

ideas.title: Interaction-Driven Simplicity: Characterizing When Sparse Feature Interactions Guarantee Reliable Simple Models
ideas.description: This research inverts the standard complexity narrative by positing that a model's reliability is not determined by its architectural simplicity alone, but by the underlying simplicity of the dominant feature interactions it captures. We develop a theoretical and empirical characterization of problem domains where the predictive signal is concentrated in a sparse set of non-linear feature interactions, rather than distributed across many individual features or dense, complex interactions. Under these conditions, we prove that simpler, inherently interpretable models (like sparse additive models or shallow trees) that explicitly represent these interactions can not only match but outperform more complete, complex models (like deep neural networks) in terms of robustness, calibration, and out-of-distribution generalization. This provides a rigorous, interaction-centric justification for model selection, moving beyond heuristic arguments to a principle grounded in the interaction structure of the data-generating process.

ideas.title: Socially-Aware Policy Optimization with Uncertainty-Weighted Preference Influence
ideas.description: This research direction integrates uncertainty-aware policy optimization with models of socially correlated user preferences. It develops a probabilistic model where user preferences are not independent but evolve within a latent social network, and the policy's interventions (e.g., recommendations, resource allocations) can actively influence this network structure and the spread of preferences. Crucially, the model quantifies epistemic uncertainty about both the current social graph and the causal effects of policy actions on it. The policy optimizer then uses these uncertainty measures to navigate a trade-off: exploiting known social influences for efficiency versus exploring cautiously to avoid triggering undesirable preference cascades or polarization, thereby enabling robust and socially responsible multi-agent policy learning.

ideas.title: Examine bias impact on real-world downstream tasks

ideas.title: Adaptive Clinical Trial Design for Risk-Aware Policy Optimization
ideas.description: This idea integrates uncertainty quantification into policy optimization by drawing an analogy from adaptive clinical trial design in biostatistics. In that field, Bayesian uncertainty measures about treatment effects are used to dynamically and ethically guide trial protocols, such as stopping rules or dose adjustments. Adapting this solution, we treat the policy learning process as an adaptive trial. The agent's probabilistic model (e.g., Bayesian neural network or probabilistic ensemble) provides real-time uncertainty estimates about value, dynamics, or reward. These uncertainty measures are then used not just for exploration, but to enforce safety and robustness through meta-actions: triggering cautious, risk-averse behavior in high-uncertainty, high-stakes situations; dynamically reallocating computational or data collection resources to reduce critical uncertainties; and implementing early rollout termination if predicted risk exceeds a safety bound. This creates a policy optimization loop that is explicitly guided by quantified uncertainty to achieve safer and more robust learning.

ideas.title: Discontinuity-Robust Preference Learning with Meta-Cognitive Regime Detection
ideas.description: This system models user preference evolution as a mixture of stable periods and abrupt transitions. A primary online inference module operates under a continuity prior, while a parallel meta-cognitive monitor tracks anomalies in feedback and engagement signals. When a potential discontinuity is detected, the system enters a confirmation phase, deploying targeted exploration and, if needed, explicit clarification queries. It then selects from or re-initializes an ensemble of latent models, allowing the policy to adapt rapidly to confirmed new regimes while maintaining robustness to noise and temporary aberrations.

ideas.title: Grounding Mixed Feedback in Objective Constraints and Subjective Preference
ideas.description: This system processes mixed feedback types by first disentangling subjective human preference signals from objective, non-negotiable task constraints and environmental physics. It maintains separate but interacting models: one for learning flexible policies from preferences and demonstrations, and another for learning inviolable constraints from negative signals, irreversible state changes, and structural task properties. A meta-controller arbitrates between these models, ensuring final behavior optimizes for stated preference only within the hard boundaries defined by the grounded constraint model, preventing the learning of preferred but impossible or dangerous actions.

ideas.title: Non-Linear Interaction Attribution via Coalitional Game Theory
ideas.description: This research direction develops a feature attribution method that inverts the standard approach by treating the model's prediction function as a non-linear value function in a cooperative game. Instead of allocating credit to individual features, the method's primary goal is to attribute the model's behavior to the network of interactions between features, such as pairwise and higher-order synergies. It achieves this by leveraging and extending solution concepts from cooperative game theory, like the Shapley interaction index or the Banzhaf interaction index, but defined for and computed directly on the model's inherent non-linearities. The result is an explanation that identifies which specific combinations of features are responsible for the model's output, providing a more accurate and interpretable map of the decision logic in complex, non-linear models like deep neural networks.

ideas.title: Adaptive Filtering for Evolving Preference Latents
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that user preference latents are static by proposing a framework where they evolve dynamically, requiring online inference and policy adaptation. Drawing an analogy from signal processing and control theory, the solution adapts the concept of the Kalman filter. In the analogous field, the Kalman filter recursively estimates the hidden state of a dynamic system from noisy observations. Adapted to this context, the user's latent preference vector is treated as the hidden state, evolving according to a defined or learned stochastic process model. An online inference algorithm, functioning like a recursive Bayesian filter, continuously updates the belief over this evolving latent state as new user interactions (observations) arrive. This enables the system's recommendation or decision policy to adapt in real-time based on the current best estimate of the user's preferences, effectively bridging the gap between a static latent model and truly dynamic user behavior.

ideas.title: Integrate uncertainty quantification into policy optimization
ideas.description: Leverage the probabilistic framework's uncertainty measures to guide safer or more robust policy learning.

ideas.title: Attributing Global Model Behavior via Statistical Mechanics and Ensemble Methods
ideas.description: This research adapts the core problem-solving framework of statistical mechanics to the challenge of attributing aggregate model behavior over a dataset. In physics, properties like temperature are explained by analyzing ensembles of particles, not individual trajectories. The analogous solution uses distribution functions and partition functions to connect microscopic states to macroscopic observables. Adapted to machine learning, we propose to treat the full dataset as a statistical ensemble and the model's processing as defining a 'computational state' for each input. The goal is to develop formal methods to define global behavioral metrics (e.g., the distribution of logits across the dataset, aggregate fairness measures, overall robustness) and then attribute changes in these aggregate metrics—induced by scaling model size, data quantity, or compute—to specific subsets or features of the data ensemble. This involves creating analogues to the partition function and performing sensitivity analyses to quantify how scaling different 'dimensions' (the SCALING_DUAL) redistributes influence across the data ensemble to drive global behavioral shifts.

ideas.title: Inverse Attribution via Constrained Optimization from Seismic Inversion
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to the inverse problem from seismic inversion in geophysics to solve the inverse attribution problem in machine learning. In the analogy, the complex, hidden model parameters and training dynamics correspond to unknown subsurface properties, and the desired attribution profile (specifying how much each input feature should contribute to the output) corresponds to a target seismic waveform. The adapted solution is to frame model training as a constrained optimization problem. A regularization term is added to the standard training loss, which measures the discrepancy between a computed attribution of the current model (using a chosen attribution method) and the desired target attribution profile. By iteratively minimizing this composite loss, the model is regularized to not only perform its primary task but also to develop internal mechanisms that yield the specified attribution behavior, effectively inverting for a model that produces the desired explanatory characteristics.

ideas.title: Active Preference Learning via Optimal Experimental Design
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from psychophysics and psychometric testing to the problem of actively learning user preferences. In psychophysics, methods like Parameter Estimation by Sequential Testing (PEST) are used to efficiently estimate a subject's sensory threshold by adaptively choosing the next stimulus based on prior responses, minimizing the number of trials. Adapted to this context, the system would actively query users not with random comparisons, but with optimally selected pairs or sets of items (e.g., product features, content options) designed to maximally reduce the entropy of the posterior distribution over the user's latent preference model. Each user interaction is treated as an informative experiment, and Bayesian optimization or information-gain criteria guide the query selection. This results in a highly sample-efficient active learning loop that rapidly infers a detailed preference profile, significantly improving personalization while minimizing user burden.

ideas.title: Structured Residual Preference Modeling via Auxiliary-Conditioned Explainers
ideas.description: This approach posits that unexplained preference variation is not noise but structured signal missed by standard latent models. It introduces a dual-component framework where a core latent factor model provides a baseline preference prediction, and a separate, flexible explainer model—conditioned on rich auxiliary user and context data—learns to predict the systematic residual. This allows the model to distinguish between true individual idiosyncrasies and predictable context-driven deviations, leading to a more complete and interpretable account of preference differences.

ideas.title: Characterizing When Simpler Preference Models Outperform Complex Social-Aware Methods
ideas.description: This research direction theoretically and empirically investigates the reliability trade-offs between simpler, independent preference models and more complex models that account for social correlations or influences within a population's latent preference space. It seeks to characterize the specific conditions—such as the strength and structure of social networks, the heterogeneity of sub-populations, or the nature of preference drift—under which the simpler, non-complete independent model proves more accurate, generalizable, or robust than its socially-aware counterpart. The core novelty is a diagnostic framework that connects the statistical properties of population-level preference dependencies to the failure modes and performance boundaries of complex latent variable models, providing principled guidance for model selection in recommender systems and algorithmic policy.

ideas.title: Circuit Breakers for Model Training: Using Attribution to Isolate and Correct Faulty Subsystems
ideas.description: This idea adapts the debugging methodology from electrical engineering circuit design to the problem of improving machine learning models. In circuit design, engineers use tools like multimeters and oscilloscopes to trace signals and isolate faulty components (e.g., a shorted capacitor) for targeted repair. Similarly, we propose using reliable attribution techniques not for post-hoc human explanation, but as an automated diagnostic system for model training. By systematically applying attribution to trace model errors and failure modes back to specific internal components (e.g., neuron clusters, attention mechanisms), we can algorithmically identify 'faulty sub-circuits.' This isolation enables precise debugging interventions—such as targeted pruning, localized retraining, or architectural patches—to correct the model's flaws, thereby improving training efficiency and final model robustness in a scalable, automated way.

ideas.title: Attribution Reliability via Structural Engineering-Inspired Sensitivity Analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts the concept of sensitivity analysis from structural engineering to define attribution reliability. In structural engineering, the robustness of a design is tested by perturbing model inputs (materials, loads) and observing the stability of stress concentrations and failure modes. Analogously, for machine learning attribution, we propose to define reliability via stability under two types of perturbations: 1) model perturbations, such as sampling from a posterior distribution of parameters or using stochastic forward passes, and 2) data perturbations, using semantically-preserving augmentations. The core metric is the sensitivity or variance of the resulting feature attribution maps; a reliable attribution for a prediction will demonstrate low sensitivity, meaning the same critical features are consistently highlighted despite small changes in the model or input, mirroring a robust engineering design where the identified failure points remain consistent under uncertainty.

ideas.title: Joint Causal Discovery and Robust Preference Learning from Feedback
ideas.description: This system addresses the fundamental problem of unknown confounding structure by not assuming a single causal graph. It first uses a minimal, targeted randomization in the user interface (e.g., randomized presentation orders or optional feature exposures) to seed causal discovery algorithms. The learned or candidate structural causal models are then tested for invariance across different user segments or environments. Finally, the method performs robust preference estimation across a set of plausible graphs, reporting a range of possible causal effects with explicit confidence bounds derived from sensitivity analysis, ensuring conclusions are not artifacts of a single untestable assumption.

ideas.title: Dynamic Multi-Resolution Interaction Discovery and Attribution
ideas.description: This research develops a formal system to efficiently discover and attribute model behavior to statistically significant feature interactions. It employs a multi-stage process that first identifies candidate interactions through sparse, regularized models and local explanation techniques. These candidates are then rigorously validated for stability and predictive power using hierarchical statistical tests across data partitions. The final output is a sparse, interpretable interaction graph that quantifies the contribution of individual features and their specific higher-order interactions, providing a complete and tractable attribution map.

ideas.title: Attributing Aggregate Model Behavior to Latent Social Preference Structures
ideas.description: This research direction develops methods to explain how and why a model behaves in a certain way across an entire population or dataset by analyzing the latent social geometry it has learned. Instead of attributing behavior to individual users or data points, we attribute it to the learned correlations, clusters, and influence networks between user preferences encoded in the model's latent space. This allows us to answer questions like whether a model's aggregate bias stems from amplifying a specific community's correlated preferences or from the structure of influence between demographic groups within its training data. The result is a macro-scale, causal understanding of model behavior rooted in the meso-scale social dynamics it has internalized.

ideas.title: Adapting Statistical Mechanics' Renormalization Group for Feature Interaction Analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts the Renormalization Group (RG) approach from statistical mechanics to challenge the assumption that model behavior is best explained by individual features. In statistical physics, RG successfully explains phase transitions by ignoring individual particles and instead analyzing how interactions between degrees of freedom change across scales. The adapted research idea is to develop a 'Feature Interaction Renormalization' framework for machine learning. This framework would systematically coarse-grain the feature space of a model, merging features and recursively quantifying how the network of interactions between them evolves. The solution identifies which higher-order interactions are 'relevant' (amplify and dominate model predictions) and which are 'irrelevant' (decay away), providing a multi-scale explanation of model behavior rooted in interaction dynamics rather than static feature importance.

ideas.title: Develop non-linear attribution via ecological interaction network analysis
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from ecology and systems biology to the problem of non-linear attribution in machine learning. In ecology, explaining complex system outcomes (like ecosystem productivity) without linear, complete decomposition is achieved by modeling species as nodes in an interaction network and analyzing the strength, modularity, and pathways of their direct and indirect effects. We propose to adapt this by treating a neural network's internal activations as nodes in a dynamic interaction graph. The goal is to develop attribution methods that quantify the non-linear, context-dependent interaction patterns (e.g., synergistic, inhibitory) between features or model components leading to a prediction, thereby providing reliable explanations that are not constrained by linearity or the requirement that attributions sum to the model's output.

ideas.title: Multi-Modal Attribution for Population-Level Behavioral Phenotypes
ideas.description: This research develops methods to attribute the aggregate predictions of multi-modal mood and stress models—which integrate data from social media, genetic markers, and traditional sources—to specific patterns within the constituent data modalities. Instead of explaining a single prediction, the goal is to explain why a model exhibits a specific behavioral pattern, such as predicting higher stress for a demographic cohort, by quantifying the contribution of, for example, a cluster of linguistic features from social media or a combination of genetic markers. This creates a novel diagnostic tool to audit complex models for population-level biases, spurious correlations, or scientifically meaningful drivers that only become apparent at scale, thereby improving model transparency and validating the utility of novel data sources for understanding group-level psychological phenomena.

ideas.title: Path-Specific Attribution in Neural Networks via Epidemiological Mediation Methods
ideas.description: This idea adapts causal mediation analysis techniques from epidemiology to the problem of interpreting complex machine learning models. In epidemiology, methods are used to decompose the effect of an exposure on an outcome into direct and indirect effects mediated through specific biological pathways, accounting for confounding. The analogous solution involves defining a formal mediation model for a neural network, where the input is the exposure, the output is the outcome, and designated internal components (e.g., specific layers, attention mechanisms, or activation patterns) are the mediators. By adapting estimators like the mediation formula or g-computation to this context, we can quantify the Path-Specific Effect (PSE)—the portion of a model's output change attributable to a causal path through a specific internal mechanism. This provides a rigorous, causal framework for attributing model behavior to its constituent parts, moving beyond correlational saliency maps to understand how information flows and is transformed along specific computational pathways within the model.

ideas.title: Robust Sensitivity Analysis for Hierarchical Latent Preference Models
ideas.description: This research direction integrates robust statistics and influence functions directly into the estimation and interpretation of complex latent preference models, such as those with hierarchical or non-Gaussian structures. It develops sensitivity measures to assess how perturbations in the observed data or model assumptions propagate through to the estimated latent variables, like individual-level hierarchies or preference clusters. This allows for a principled, diagnostic tool to distinguish core, stable features of the latent preference space from fragile or overly data-sensitive constructs. Consequently, the approach provides a statistically grounded method for validating the complexity of a learned preference model, preventing over-interpretation of spurious latent patterns.
